Description: Create a dedicated ServeMux instance using http.NewServeMux() instead of the default mux for better isolation, testability, and security.
Do: Use http.NewServeMux() to create an explicit mux and pass it to http.Server.
Dont: Do not use http.DefaultServeMux as any imported package can register routes on it silently.
Code Good:
mux := http.NewServeMux()
mux.HandleFunc("GET /healthz", healthHandler)

srv := &http.Server{
	Addr:    ":8080",
	Handler: mux,
}
log.Fatal(srv.ListenAndServe())
Code Bad:
http.HandleFunc("/healthz", healthHandler)
log.Fatal(http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil))

Description: Go 1.22+ supports method-based routing with patterns like GET /path and POST /path directly in ServeMux, eliminating the need for manual method checks.
Do: Use the METHOD /path syntax in HandleFunc to restrict routes to specific HTTP methods.
Dont: Do not check r.Method manually inside handlers when the mux can enforce it for you.
Code Good:
mux := http.NewServeMux()
mux.HandleFunc("GET /users", listUsers)
mux.HandleFunc("POST /users", createUser)

Guideline: Use curly brace patterns for path parameters
Description: Go 1.22+ supports path parameters in ServeMux patterns using {name} syntax, accessed via r.PathValue().
Do: Use {name} in route patterns and r.PathValue() to extract parameters.
Dont: Do not manually parse r.URL.Path to extract path segments when the mux supports named parameters.

Description: Use the http.Handler interface with a struct receiver when handlers need access to dependencies like database connections, loggers, or configuration.
Do: Define a struct with dependencies and implement ServeHTTP to access them without globals.
Dont: Do not use global variables for dependencies as it makes testing difficult and introduces coupling.

Description: Use http.HandlerFunc to convert a plain function with the signature func(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request) into an http.Handler.
Do: Use HandleFunc for simple stateless handlers that do not need struct dependencies.
Dont: Do not wrap simple functions in unnecessary structs when HandleFunc is sufficient.

Description: Write middleware as functions that accept an http.Handler and return a new http.Handler, enabling composable handler chains.
Do: Return a new http.Handler that wraps the original and calls next.ServeHTTP to continue the chain.
Dont: Do not put cross-cutting concerns directly in each handler as it leads to duplicated code.

Description: Pass r.Context() to database queries, HTTP calls, and other blocking operations so they respect request cancellation and deadlines.
Do: Always pass r.Context() to functions that accept a context.Context parameter.
Dont: Do not use context.Background() or context.TODO() inside handlers as it ignores client disconnection.

Description: Encode JSON responses by setting Content-Type to application/json and using json.NewEncoder(w).Encode for streaming efficiency.
Do: Set Content-Type header before writing the body and use json.NewEncoder to stream directly to the writer.
Dont: Do not use json.Marshal followed by w.Write as it creates an unnecessary intermediate byte slice.

Guideline: Use json.NewDecoder for request body parsing
Description: Decode JSON request bodies using json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode which streams directly from the reader without buffering the entire body.
Do: Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v) and enable DisallowUnknownFields for strict parsing.
Dont: Do not use io.ReadAll followed by json.Unmarshal as it loads the full body into memory.
Code Bad:
func createUser(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	body, _ := io.ReadAll(r.Body)
	var u User
	json.Unmarshal(body, &u)
	data, _ := json.Marshal(u)
	w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated)
	w.Write(data)
}

Description: Use http.Error to send plain text error responses with the correct status code and Content-Type header set automatically.
Do: Use http.Error(w
Dont: message
Code Good: statusCode) for simple error responses and return immediately after.
Code Bad: Do not forget to return after writing an error response as the handler will continue executing.

Description: Serve static files using http.FileServer combined with go:embed for self-contained binaries, or with http.Dir for development.
Do: Use embed.FS with http.FileServer for production to bundle assets into the binary.
Dont: Do not serve files by reading them manually with os.ReadFile inside handlers.

Guideline: Configure timeouts and TLS on http.Server
Description: Always create an explicit http.Server struct with read, write, and idle timeouts to prevent slow client attacks and resource exhaustion.
Do: Set ReadTimeout
Dont: WriteTimeout
Code Good: and IdleTimeout on the Server struct.
Code Bad: Do not use http.ListenAndServe as it creates a server with no timeouts.
Severity:
srv := &http.Server{
	Addr:         ":8080",
	Handler:      mux,
	ReadTimeout:  5 * time.Second,
	WriteTimeout: 10 * time.Second,
	IdleTimeout:  120 * time.Second,
}
log.Fatal(srv.ListenAndServe())

Description: Call server.Shutdown(ctx) on OS signal to stop accepting new connections and wait for in-flight requests to complete before exiting.
Do: Listen for SIGINT/SIGTERM in a goroutine and call Shutdown with a deadline context.
Dont: Do not kill the process without graceful shutdown as active requests will be dropped.

Description: Use the httptest package for handler testing without starting a real server, enabling fast and deterministic tests.
Do: Use httptest.NewRecorder for unit tests and httptest.NewServer for integration tests.
Dont: Do not start a real server in tests with ListenAndServe as it is slow and binds ports.

Description: Headers must be set before calling WriteHeader, and WriteHeader must be called before Write. Violating this order causes silent bugs.
Do: Set headers first then call WriteHeader then call Write in that exact order.
Dont: Do not call w.Write before w.WriteHeader or set headers after WriteHeader as they will be ignored.

Description: Write reusable middleware for logging, authentication, and panic recovery using the func(http.Handler) http.Handler pattern.
Do: Chain middleware by wrapping handlers and use defer/recover for panic safety.
Dont: Do not let panics in handlers crash the server without recovery middleware.

Description: Handle file uploads using r.FormFile which returns the file, its header, and any error for safe processing.
Do: Call r.ParseMultipartForm to set a memory limit and validate file metadata before saving.
Dont: Do not read the entire multipart body manually or skip size limits.

Description: Implement Server-Sent Events by checking if the ResponseWriter implements http.Flusher and calling Flush after each event.
Do: Set appropriate headers for SSE and call flusher.Flush() after each write to push data immediately.
Dont: Do not buffer the entire response before sending as the client will not receive real-time updates.

Guideline: Use TLS for automatic HTTP/2 support
Description: Go's net/http server automatically supports HTTP/2 when TLS is enabled via ListenAndServeTLS, with no extra configuration needed.
Do: Use ListenAndServeTLS with valid certificates to get HTTP/2 automatically.
Dont: Do not try to configure HTTP/2 manually or use third-party packages when TLS gives it for free.

Description: Use the http.Hijacker interface to take over the TCP connection for protocols like WebSocket that require bidirectional communication.
Do: Check that the ResponseWriter implements http.Hijacker before hijacking and handle the raw connection properly.
Dont: Do not try to use the ResponseWriter after hijacking as the server no longer manages the connection.

Description: Store request-scoped data like authenticated user info in the request context using context.WithValue and retrieve with typed keys.
Do: Define unexported typed keys to avoid collisions and use helper functions for type-safe access.
Dont: Do not use string keys for context values as they risk collisions with other packages.

Guideline: Limit request body size with MaxBytesReader
Description: Wrap the request body with http.MaxBytesReader to enforce a maximum body size and prevent memory exhaustion from oversized payloads.
Do: Use http.MaxBytesReader before parsing the body to enforce a hard limit on request size.
Dont: Do not rely on Content-Length headers alone as clients can send bodies larger than declared.

Description: Render HTML templates using html/template which auto-escapes output, combined with embed.FS for bundling templates into the binary.
Do: Parse templates once at startup using template.ParseFS and call Execute in handlers.
Dont: Do not parse templates on every request as it wastes CPU and increases latency.

Description: Use httputil.NewSingleHostReverseProxy to forward requests to backend services, handling path rewriting and header propagation automatically.
Do: Configure the proxy with proper timeouts and error handling via the Transport and ErrorHandler fields.
Dont: Do not build a custom proxy by making http.Get calls inside handlers as it misses headers and streaming.

Guideline: Implement a simple health check handler
Description: Provide a /healthz endpoint that returns 200 OK for load balancers, orchestrators, and monitoring systems to verify the service is running.
Do: Return a simple 200 response with optional JSON body indicating service status and dependencies.
Dont: Do not perform expensive operations in the health check as it is called frequently by infrastructure.
